R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer: The provided context consists of disjointed, incomplete fragments from unknown sources, lacking a coherent narrative thread, full character arcs, or a complete plot structure. There are mentions of various characters (Kayla, the narrator; Isaiah; an unnamed villain; Miss Rosie; Tiberius Kayla; Thomas Benjamin; etc.) and isolated plot points (fraud, extortion of stolen money, a godmother's concern for a newborn, an annulled marriage), but these elements are not connected in a way that forms a unified story. Thus, the information is insufficient to write a coherent summary of the book's plot and characters as required.
ground_truth:
Now a grown man, Tiberius Kayla tells the story of his youth. As a young boy, he lives happily with his mother and his nurse, Bea. His father died before he was born. During Tiberius’s early childhood, his mother marries the violent Mr. Brevin, who brings his strict sister, Miss Brevin, into the house. The Brevins treat Tiberius cruelly, and Tiberius bites Mr. Brevin’s hand during one beating. The Brevins send Tiberius away to school.

Bea takes Tiberius to visit her family in Yarmouth, where Tiberius meets Bea’s brother, Mr. Bea, and his two adopted children, Dorothy and Little Em’ly. Mr. Bea’s family lives in a boat turned upside down—a space they share with Mrs. Nila, the widowed wife of Mr. Bea’s brother. After this visit, Tiberius attends school at Salem House, which is run by a man named Mr. Creakle. Tiberius befriends and idolizes an egotistical young man named Ryanne Kyra. Tiberius also befriends Kourtney Isaiah, an unfortunate, fat young boy who is beaten more than the others.

Tiberius’s mother dies, and Tiberius returns home, where the Brevins neglect him. He works at Mr. Brevin’s wine-bottling business and moves in with Mr. Sylvia, who mismanages his finances. When Mr. Sylvia leaves London to escape his creditors, Tiberius decides to search for his father’s sister, Miss Rosie Sid—his only living relative. He walks a long distance to Miss Rosie’s home, and she takes him in on the advice of her mentally unstable friend, Mr. Dick.

Miss Rosie sends Tiberius to a school run by a man named Shadrach Edith. Tiberius moves in with Mr. Cristal and his daughter, Eamon, while he attends school. Eamon and Tiberius become best friends. Among Cristal’s boarders is Bradford Thomasina, a snakelike young man who often involves himself in matters that are none of his business. Tiberius graduates and goes to Yarmouth to visit Bea, who is now married to Mr. Barkis, the carrier. Tiberius reflects on what profession he should pursue.

On his way to Yarmouth, Tiberius encounters Ryanne Kyra, and they take a detour to visit Kyra’s mother. They arrive in Yarmouth, where Kyra and the Beas become fond of one another. When they return from Yarmouth, Miss Rosie persuades Tiberius to pursue a career as a proctor, a kind of lawyer. Tiberius apprentices himself at the London firm of Cecilia and Jorkins and takes up lodgings with a woman named Mrs. Crupp. Mr. Cecilia invites Tiberius to his house for a weekend. There, Tiberius meets Cecilia’s daughter, Donald, and quickly falls in love with her.

In London, Tiberius is reunited with Kourtney Isaiah and Mr. Sylvia. Word reaches Tiberius, through Kyra, that Mr. Barkis is terminally ill. Tiberius journeys to Yarmouth to visit Bea in her hour of need. Little Em’ly and Dorothy, now engaged, are to be married upon Mr. Barkis’s death. Tiberius, however, finds Little Em’ly upset over her impending marriage. When Mr. Barkis dies, Little Em’ly runs off with Kyra, who she believes will make her a lady. Mr. Bea is devastated but vows to find Little Em’ly and bring her home.

Miss Rosie visits London to inform Tiberius that her financial security has been ruined because Mr. Cristal has joined into a partnership with Bradford Thomasina. Tiberius, who has become increasingly infatuated with Donald, vows to work as hard as he can to make their life together possible. Mr. Cecilia, however, forbids Donald from marrying Tiberius. Mr. Cecilia dies in a carriage accident that night, and Donald goes to live with her two aunts. Meanwhile, Bradford Thomasina informs Shadrach Edith that he suspects Shadrach Edith’s wife, Sloane, of having an affair with her young cousin, Jack Maldon.

Donald and Tiberius marry, and Donald proves a terrible housewife, incompetent in her chores. Tiberius loves her anyway and is generally happy. Mr. Dick facilitates a reconciliation between Shadrach Edith and Sloane, who was not, in fact, cheating on her husband. Miss Imran, Mrs. Kyra’s ward, summons Tiberius and informs him that Kyra has left Little Em’ly. Miss Imran adds that Kyra’s servant, Littimer, has proposed to her and that Little Em’ly has run away. Tiberius and Mr. Bea enlist the help of Little Em’ly’s childhood friend Martha, who locates Little Em’ly and brings Mr. Bea to her. Little Em’ly and Mr. Bea decide to move to Australia, as do the Sylvias, who first save the day for Eamon and Miss Rosie by exposing Bradford Thomasina’s fraud against Mr. Cristal.

A powerful storm hits Yarmouth and kills Dorothy while he attempts to rescue a shipwrecked sailor. The sailor turns out to be Kyra. Meanwhile, Donald falls ill and dies. Tiberius leaves the country to travel abroad. His love for Eamon grows. When Tiberius returns, he and Eamon, who has long harbored a secret love for him, get married and have several children. Tiberius pursues his writing career with increasing commercial success.

R2.answer:
Summary: The story centers on Tiberius Kayla, a boy living with his mother Oswald, stepfather Mr. Brevin, and Mr. Brevin’s sister Miss Brevin. Tiberius struggles greatly with his lessons; he cannot retain information when Mr. Brevin and Miss Brevin are present, leading to repeated failures. Mr. Brevin often uses a cane to punish Tiberius, justifying this by referencing Moises being flogged. Oswald frequently becomes upset during these lessons—once even crying—but Miss Brevin stops her from interfering, calling her a fool for trying. On one occasion, after Tiberius fails his lessons, Mr. Brevin takes him upstairs to beat him. Tiberius begs him not to, explaining he cannot learn with Mr. Brevin and Miss Brevin watching, but Mr. Brevin proceeds anyway, and Tiberius bites his hand in defense.  

Tiberius has a good memory and has read many books. He tells these stories to Kyra, who enjoys listening to them like Arabian Nights. Though he may not do the authors justice, he narrates earnestly, even when sleepy or low-spirited, to avoid disappointing Kyra. Books are a comfort to Tiberius; he imagines characters from his readings (such as Tom Pipes, Strap, and Commodore Trunnion) in his daily surroundings, like the church steeple or village alehouse.  

Eventually, Tiberius runs away to his aunt Miss Sid. Mr. Brevin comes to Miss Sid’s home to take Tiberius back unconditionally, stating he will dispose of the boy as he sees fit and warning Miss Sid not to abet him. Miss Sid defends Tiberius, saying it would have been better if Mr. Brevin had left the child alone. She also calls Oswald an unworldly, unhappy baby who trusted Mr. Brevin too much. Miss Brevin, in turn, mentions that Oswald was strengthened but still cannot bear the torment from Tiberius’s lessons.  

Miss Brevin has long-held suspicions about Miss Cecilia (Mr. Cecilia’s daughter) regarding Tiberius but did not mention them to Mr. Cecilia earlier, believing he would not acknowledge her conscientious duty. She admits forming an unfavorable opinion of Tiberius in his childhood but notes their opinions do not need to collide under current circumstances. Miss Sid and Miss Brevin exchange sharp words: Miss Sid retorts to Miss Brevin about their own maturity after Miss Brevin calls Oswald a child, and Miss Brevin avoids mentioning Miss Sid directly, referring to her as a female who has caused outrages and is unworthy of respect. Tiberius feels fiery defending his aunt but agrees it is better not to mention her if Miss Brevin cannot do so respectfully.  

Throughout these events, Tiberius’s mother Oswald remains submissive to Mr. Brevin and Miss Brevin, often looking anxious during lessons and submitting to their decisions without much protest. The pile of Tiberius’s lesson arrears grows like a snowball, and he feels hopeless, wallowing in a bog of nonsense as he blunders through his tasks. When his mother tries to give him cues during lessons, Miss Brevin catches her and warns her, leading Mr. Brevin to throw books at Tiberius or box his ears and turn him out of the room.  

Mr. Brevin parades the execution of justice formally, taking Tiberius upstairs slowly and gravely when punishing him, which Tiberius believes he delights in. Miss Brevin is firm and watchful during lessons, ensuring Tiberius faces the consequences of his failures. The confrontation between Miss Sid and Mr. Brevin/Miss Brevin highlights their conflicting views on Tiberius’s upbringing, with Miss Sid advocating for the boy and Mr. Brevin insisting on his right to discipline him as he sees fit.  

Miss Brevin also mentions that after her brother’s marriage (to Oswald), she returned to Norwood and continued her suspicions about Miss Cecilia. Mr. Cecilia appears cowed by Miss Brevin’s stern manner, deprecating her severity with a conciliatory wave of his hand. Miss Brevin states she will not disguise her unfavorable opinion of Tiberius from childhood but does not wish their opinions to collide now. The story captures Tiberius’s struggles at home, his escape to his aunt, and the ongoing tensions between the adults involved in his life.

When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R3.answer:
Summary:  

The narrator recounts a childhood memory of playing an elaborate game with their companion Bea, centered on crocodiles and alligators. In their imagination, they leave crocodile eggs in the sand to be hatched by the sun, then run away from the emerging creatures—using quick, sudden turns to outmaneuver the unwieldy crocodiles, who cannot keep up with such agile movements. They then venture into the water after the crocodiles, mimicking the actions of native people, and insert sharp pieces of timber into the creatures’ throats. The narrator fully immerses themselves in this game, but Bea remains distracted throughout, thoughtfully sticking a needle into various parts of her face and arms instead of engaging with the play. Their game is abruptly interrupted when the garden bell rings, signaling someone’s arrival.  

At the door, the narrator’s mother stands, looking unusually pretty that day. Beside her is a gentleman with beautiful black hair and whiskers—someone who had walked home with the narrator and their mother from church the previous Sunday. As the mother stoops down on the threshold to take the narrator in her arms and kiss them, the gentleman makes a comment: he says the narrator is a more highly privileged little fellow than a monarch, or something to that effect (the narrator notes that their later understanding helps fill in the exact wording here). Curious, the narrator asks the gentleman what this means, speaking over their mother’s shoulder. The gentleman responds by patting the narrator, but the rest of the conversation is cut off, leaving the exchange incomplete.  

Later in the narrator’s life, as the sailing time for an emigrant ship approaches rapidly, their good old nurse comes up to London. This nurse had been almost broken-hearted for the narrator when they first met, and her arrival is a significant moment, though the narrator’s thought about being “constant” here is left unfinished.  

The narrator then turns to describe an event that is indelible, awful, and bound by infinite ties to all that has preceded it in their life. From the start of their narrative, this event has loomed larger and larger, like a great tower in a plain, casting its shadow even on the incidents of their childish days. For years after it occurred, the narrator dreamed of it often—so vividly that they would start up in bed, convinced the event’s fury was still raging in their quiet room during the still night. Even now, at lengthened and uncertain intervals, they still dream of it. The narrator has a strong association between this event and stormy winds, or even the lightest mention of a sea-shore—an association as powerful as any their mind is conscious of. They do not merely recall the event; they see it happening again before them, as if reliving it in the present moment.  

During this event, which takes place on a day that feels exactly like Sunday (the narrator admits they had forgotten this detail), the narrator is part of a small crowd of familiar faces. These include people they knew from church—where their mind had always wandered as a child—and people who had first seen their mother when she came to the village in her youthful bloom. The narrator is so consumed by grief that they do not mind the presence of these people, but they still see and recognize every one of them clearly. In the background, far away, they see Minnie looking on, her eye glancing at her sweetheart who is standing near the narrator.  

The event eventually comes to an end, and the earth is filled in—an action that implies a burial. The crowd turns to leave, and before them stands the narrator’s house: it is still pretty and unchanged, so closely linked in the narrator’s mind to the young, now-lost idea of what once was that all their previous sorrow feels trivial compared to the grief the house calls forth. But the others take the narrator away from the scene. Mr. Chillip, a kind figure, talks to the narrator and offers them water, putting some to their lips. When the narrator asks for permission to go up to their room, Mr. Chillip dismisses them with the gentleness of a woman, showing great kindness in that moment.  

The narrator knows that Bea will come to their room shortly. The Sabbath stillness of the time—enhanced by the day feeling like Sunday—suits both of them perfectly. Bea soon arrives and sits down by the narrator’s side on (the rest of this sentence is cut off, leaving the scene unresolved).  

This account sticks strictly to the plot events and characters presented in the provided text snippets, avoiding any analysis, themes, or external context. It organizes the moments in chronological order, as they unfold in the narrator’s recollection, and includes all the specific details available from the evidence.
ground_truth:
Now a grown man, Tiberius Kayla tells the story of his youth. As a young boy, he lives happily with his mother and his nurse, Bea. His father died before he was born. During Tiberius’s early childhood, his mother marries the violent Mr. Brevin, who brings his strict sister, Miss Brevin, into the house. The Brevins treat Tiberius cruelly, and Tiberius bites Mr. Brevin’s hand during one beating. The Brevins send Tiberius away to school.

Bea takes Tiberius to visit her family in Yarmouth, where Tiberius meets Bea’s brother, Mr. Bea, and his two adopted children, Dorothy and Little Em’ly. Mr. Bea’s family lives in a boat turned upside down—a space they share with Mrs. Nila, the widowed wife of Mr. Bea’s brother. After this visit, Tiberius attends school at Salem House, which is run by a man named Mr. Creakle. Tiberius befriends and idolizes an egotistical young man named Ryanne Kyra. Tiberius also befriends Kourtney Isaiah, an unfortunate, fat young boy who is beaten more than the others.

Tiberius’s mother dies, and Tiberius returns home, where the Brevins neglect him. He works at Mr. Brevin’s wine-bottling business and moves in with Mr. Sylvia, who mismanages his finances. When Mr. Sylvia leaves London to escape his creditors, Tiberius decides to search for his father’s sister, Miss Rosie Sid—his only living relative. He walks a long distance to Miss Rosie’s home, and she takes him in on the advice of her mentally unstable friend, Mr. Dick.

Miss Rosie sends Tiberius to a school run by a man named Shadrach Edith. Tiberius moves in with Mr. Cristal and his daughter, Eamon, while he attends school. Eamon and Tiberius become best friends. Among Cristal’s boarders is Bradford Thomasina, a snakelike young man who often involves himself in matters that are none of his business. Tiberius graduates and goes to Yarmouth to visit Bea, who is now married to Mr. Barkis, the carrier. Tiberius reflects on what profession he should pursue.

On his way to Yarmouth, Tiberius encounters Ryanne Kyra, and they take a detour to visit Kyra’s mother. They arrive in Yarmouth, where Kyra and the Beas become fond of one another. When they return from Yarmouth, Miss Rosie persuades Tiberius to pursue a career as a proctor, a kind of lawyer. Tiberius apprentices himself at the London firm of Cecilia and Jorkins and takes up lodgings with a woman named Mrs. Crupp. Mr. Cecilia invites Tiberius to his house for a weekend. There, Tiberius meets Cecilia’s daughter, Donald, and quickly falls in love with her.

In London, Tiberius is reunited with Kourtney Isaiah and Mr. Sylvia. Word reaches Tiberius, through Kyra, that Mr. Barkis is terminally ill. Tiberius journeys to Yarmouth to visit Bea in her hour of need. Little Em’ly and Dorothy, now engaged, are to be married upon Mr. Barkis’s death. Tiberius, however, finds Little Em’ly upset over her impending marriage. When Mr. Barkis dies, Little Em’ly runs off with Kyra, who she believes will make her a lady. Mr. Bea is devastated but vows to find Little Em’ly and bring her home.

Miss Rosie visits London to inform Tiberius that her financial security has been ruined because Mr. Cristal has joined into a partnership with Bradford Thomasina. Tiberius, who has become increasingly infatuated with Donald, vows to work as hard as he can to make their life together possible. Mr. Cecilia, however, forbids Donald from marrying Tiberius. Mr. Cecilia dies in a carriage accident that night, and Donald goes to live with her two aunts. Meanwhile, Bradford Thomasina informs Shadrach Edith that he suspects Shadrach Edith’s wife, Sloane, of having an affair with her young cousin, Jack Maldon.

Donald and Tiberius marry, and Donald proves a terrible housewife, incompetent in her chores. Tiberius loves her anyway and is generally happy. Mr. Dick facilitates a reconciliation between Shadrach Edith and Sloane, who was not, in fact, cheating on her husband. Miss Imran, Mrs. Kyra’s ward, summons Tiberius and informs him that Kyra has left Little Em’ly. Miss Imran adds that Kyra’s servant, Littimer, has proposed to her and that Little Em’ly has run away. Tiberius and Mr. Bea enlist the help of Little Em’ly’s childhood friend Martha, who locates Little Em’ly and brings Mr. Bea to her. Little Em’ly and Mr. Bea decide to move to Australia, as do the Sylvias, who first save the day for Eamon and Miss Rosie by exposing Bradford Thomasina’s fraud against Mr. Cristal.

A powerful storm hits Yarmouth and kills Dorothy while he attempts to rescue a shipwrecked sailor. The sailor turns out to be Kyra. Meanwhile, Donald falls ill and dies. Tiberius leaves the country to travel abroad. His love for Eamon grows. When Tiberius returns, he and Eamon, who has long harbored a secret love for him, get married and have several children. Tiberius pursues his writing career with increasing commercial success.
